otRoot.heading: The OT Root: Isaiah's Invitation
otRoot.text: Seven centuries before Andrew heard those words, Isaiah wrote: 'Come, all you who are thirsty, come to the waters... give ear and come to me; listen, that you may live.' (Isaiah 55:1-3). Jesus didn't invent the invitation — he IS the fulfillment of it.

wordStudy.heading: Hebrew: בֹּאוּ (Bo'u) — 'Come'
wordStudy.text: The Hebrew word for 'come' in Isaiah is bo'u — an urgent plural imperative. Not a polite suggestion. A summons. When Jesus says 'Come and see,' he is echoing a divine invitation woven through the whole Hebrew Bible.

narrativeArc.heading: The Grand Story: The God Who Seeks
narrativeArc.text: From Genesis (God calling 'Where are you?' to Adam) to Revelation (the Spirit and Bride saying 'Come!'), the entire Bible is the story of God pursuing humanity. Andrew's story is one scene in that pursuit.

newCreation.heading: New Creation Echo
newCreation.text: The disciples leaving their nets echoes Ezekiel's vision: 'I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit in you.' (Ezekiel 36:26). The old life isn't just left — it's transformed into something entirely new.

otRoot.heading: The OT Root: Isaiah's Fishing Prophecy
otRoot.text: Jeremiah 16:16 foretells: 'I will send for many fishermen... and they will catch them.' But the deeper root is in Ezekiel 47 — a river flowing from the Temple into the Dead Sea, teeming with fish. Life coming from the holy place outward.

wordStudy.heading: Greek: ἁλιεῖς ἀνθρώπων — 'Fishers of People'
wordStudy.text: The Greek word for fish (ἁλιεύς, halieus) was also used metaphorically in Greco-Roman culture for gathering people. But Jesus reframes it entirely — not catching to consume, but gathering to restore.

narrativeArc.heading: Simon → Peter: The Name Change
narrativeArc.text: Name changes in scripture signal covenant transformation. Abram → Abraham. Jacob → Israel. Now Simon → Cephas (Rock). God doesn't just call you — he redefines who you are.

newCreation.heading: New Creation Echo
newCreation.text: The miraculous catch foreshadows the end of John's Gospel — another miraculous catch, on the same Sea of Galilee, after the Resurrection. Peter's story begins and ends with an impossible net full of fish.

otRoot.heading: The OT Root: Ezekiel's New Spirit
otRoot.text: Jesus isn't introducing a new idea to Nicodemus — he's indicting him for missing it. Ezekiel 36:25-27 already promised: 'I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit in you... I will put my Spirit in you.' A Pharisee would know this text. Jesus asks: 'You are Israel's teacher and you don't understand this?'

wordStudy.heading: Greek: ἄνωθεν (anothen) — 'Again' or 'From Above'
wordStudy.text: The word anothen is deliberately ambiguous — it means BOTH 'again' AND 'from above.' Nicodemus hears 'again' (second birth). Jesus means 'from above' (divine birth). The double meaning is the whole point. The new birth isn't a second effort — it's an entirely different SOURCE.

narrativeArc.heading: The Bronze Serpent: Moses in the Wilderness
narrativeArc.text: Jesus references Numbers 21 — the bronze serpent lifted up on a pole so Israel could look and be healed from snakebite. 'Just as Moses lifted up the snake in the wilderness, so the Son of Man must be lifted up.' The cross is the ultimate 'look and live' moment.

newCreation.heading: New Creation Echo
newCreation.text: Wind/Spirit (ruach) appears first in Genesis 1:2 — hovering over the formless void before creation. Jesus says the Spirit/wind blows wherever it pleases. New birth is new creation. The same Spirit who made the world makes you new.

deepDive.heading: Nicodemus: The Arc of a Pharisee
deepDive.text: Nicodemus appears three times in John's Gospel. Here — secret, in darkness. Later (7:50) — cautiously defending Jesus before the council. At the cross (19:39) — bringing 75 pounds of burial spices, publicly. His journey from night to day mirrors the theme of John's Gospel itself: light coming into darkness.

otRoot.heading: The OT Root: Elijah's Cloak on Elisha
otRoot.text: When Elijah found Elisha plowing with twelve yoke of oxen (1 Kings 19:19–21), he threw his cloak over him without a word. Elisha ran, asked only to say goodbye, then slaughtered his oxen and burned the plow — an irreversible act. There was no going back. The same urgency echoes centuries later on the shore of Galilee: a cloak thrown, nets abandoned, a life surrendered on the spot.

wordStudy.heading: Greek: ἀκολουθέω (akoloutheo) — 'To Follow'
wordStudy.text: Akoloutheo carries the sense of walking the same road as another — conforming your steps to theirs. It is the word of apprenticeship, of loyalty, of choosing one direction over all others. In the Synoptic Gospels it is nearly always used of following Jesus specifically, never casually. To 'follow' in this sense is not to trail behind — it is to reorient the whole self.

narrativeArc.heading: The Grand Story: Rupture, Not Recruitment
narrativeArc.text: The calling of Andrew, Peter, James, and John is not a recruitment — it is a rupture. Jesus does not explain his credentials, outline a mission statement, or offer incentives. He speaks and walks. What compels a man to drop his livelihood, leave his father in the boat, and go? The text does not say. The silence is the point: something in the call itself carries authority that argument never could.

newCreation.heading: New Creation Echo: From Nets to Nations
newCreation.text: Fishermen hauling nets from the Sea of Galilee prefigures the final scene of Revelation — every tongue and tribe gathered before the throne. The same hands that once sorted fish by species will one day be part of a harvest that spans the world. Small beginnings, cosmic ends.

scroll_water_to_wine.title: The First Sign — Water into Wine

otRoot.heading: The OT Root: Elisha and the Bitter Water
otRoot.text: In 2 Kings 2:19–22, Elisha throws salt into a polluted spring and declares the water healed — useless water made pure by prophetic word. The pattern is older still: Moses struck the rock and water flowed; Ezekiel saw a river from the Temple turning the Dead Sea fresh. Jesus at Cana stands in this line, but surpasses it. He does not heal what is broken — he transforms the ordinary into abundance.

wordStudy.heading: Hebrew: יַיִן / מַיִם (Yayin / Mayim) — Wine and Water
wordStudy.text: The Hebrew words for wine (yayin) and water (mayim) are near-homophones — a sonic echo buried in the language itself, as if the tongue always half-expected the transformation. Water is the substance of creation's first day; wine is the symbol of covenant joy, of the messianic banquet Isaiah promises. Jesus turning one into the other is not a party trick — it is a declaration.

narrativeArc.heading: The Grand Story: From Void to Abundance
narrativeArc.text: In Genesis, the deep waters were formless and empty — the raw material of a creation not yet spoken into order. At Cana, six stone water jars stand empty. Jesus fills them, then exceeds all expectation: 120 gallons of the finest wine. The same movement runs from Genesis to Revelation — God taking what is empty, formless, or bitter and making it overflow with life.

newCreation.heading: New Creation Echo: The Wedding Banquet
newCreation.text: The wedding at Cana is a preview of the Marriage Supper of the Lamb. Isaiah 25:6 promises God will prepare 'a feast of rich food for all peoples, a banquet of aged wine.' Jesus does not wait until the end of history to begin the party — he starts it in Galilee, at a village wedding, with six ordinary stone jars.

scroll_authority.title: With Authority — Not Like the Scribes

otRoot.heading: The OT Root: Moses and the Scribes
otRoot.text: Moses spoke to Israel as the one who had been in God's presence — not quoting predecessors but declaring what he had received directly (Deuteronomy 5:4). The scribes, by contrast, derived their authority from the chain of tradition: 'Rabbi Shammai says... Rabbi Hillel says...' Jesus cuts through all of it. He says 'I say to you' — not 'it is written' as citation, but as the one who wrote it.

wordStudy.heading: Greek: ἐξουσία (exousia) — Authority
wordStudy.text: Exousia means authority that comes from one's own being — not delegated power but inherent right. The scribes had authority on loan, derived from tradition. Jesus has exousia as one who possesses it himself. When the unclean spirit obeys, it is not following a ritual formula — it is responding to a Person who has authority over it by nature.

narrativeArc.heading: The Grand Story: The Word Who Speaks
narrativeArc.text: At Creation, God's word had authority because it was creative speech — 'Let there be light,' and there was. John opens his Gospel: 'In the beginning was the Word.' Jesus in the synagogue is not a man with impressive doctrine. He is the Word made flesh, speaking into chaos as he did at the dawn of time. The unclean spirit recognizes what the crowds only sense: something fundamental has entered the room.

newCreation.heading: New Creation Echo: Chaos Brought to Order
newCreation.text: The possessed man in the synagogue mirrors the formless void — chaos inhabiting what should be a house of order and worship. Jesus speaks, and chaos yields. This is the pattern of all new creation: not just healing the individual, but restoring the whole space to the purpose for which it was made.

otRoot.heading: The OT Root: Moses, Jacob, and the Well
otRoot.text: In biblical narrative, a meeting at a well is a betrothal scene (see: Isaac & Rebekah, Jacob & Rachel, Moses & Zipporah). Jesus meeting the Samaritan woman at Jacob's well is not accidental. John is signaling: the Bridegroom has come to claim his bride — even the outcasts, even the broken ones.

wordStudy.heading: Hebrew: מַיִם חַיִּים (Mayim Chayyim) — 'Living Water'
wordStudy.text: In Jewish culture, 'living water' meant flowing water — a spring, not a cistern. It was ritually pure. The Mishnah required it for mikveh (ritual immersion). Jesus uses a technical term — then transforms it completely. He IS the living water. Not a ritual. A person.

narrativeArc.heading: Thirst: From Eden to Revelation
narrativeArc.text: The theme of thirst runs from Genesis (the desert wandering, the rock that gave water) through Isaiah 55 ('Come, all who are thirsty') to the last lines of Revelation: 'Let the one who is thirsty come; and let the one who wishes take the free gift of the water of life.' The Samaritan woman is the whole human story.

newCreation.heading: New Creation Echo: The River of Life
newCreation.text: Ezekiel 47 and Revelation 22 both describe a river of life flowing from the presence of God — trees lining its banks, healing the nations. When Jesus says the living water will become 'a spring welling up to eternal life,' he is describing the New Creation happening INSIDE the person who believes.
